explanation: The phrase "Once prop_0, it shall be possible to prop_1" means that whenever prop_0 becomes true, it must be the case that prop_1 eventually becomes true from that point onwards. "Shall be possible to prop_1" translates to 'eventually prop_1' (F prop_1). The 'once' implies a universal temporal implication: 'Always, if prop_0 is true, then eventually prop_1'. This is captured by G (prop_0 -> F prop_1).

The statement "Once prop_0, it shall be possible to prop_1" means that at any point in time, if prop_0 becomes true, then it must eventually be possible for prop_1 to become true from that point onwards. This is captured by the LTL formula G (prop_0 -> F prop_1), which reads "Globally, if prop_0 is true, then eventually prop_1 will be true."

The statement "Once prop_0, it shall be possible to prop_1" means that if prop_0 happens at any point in time, then at some point in time (possibly the same point) prop_1 must also happen. This is a direct implication between two eventualities.

The statement "Once prop_0, prop_1" implies a global conditional relationship. It means that whenever 'prop_0' is true, 'prop_1' must also be true at that exact same moment. This is best represented by a global implication.

The phrase "Once prop_0" implies that if 'prop_0' becomes true at any point in time, then the subsequent condition must hold from that point onward. The condition "it shall be possible to prop_1" means that 'prop_1' must eventually become true. Therefore, for any point in time, if 'prop_0' is true at that point, it implies that 'prop_1' will eventually be true. This is expressed using the global operator 'G' and the implication '->' leading to 'F' (eventually).

The phrase "Once prop_0, it shall be possible to prop_1" implies that if prop_0 occurs at some point, then from that point onwards, it must be possible for prop_1 to occur. This is best captured by the 'F (A & F B)' pattern in LTL, which means 'Eventually, A is true AND from that time, eventually B is true'. This ensures that prop_1 happens at or after prop_0.

The statement "Once prop_0, it shall be possible to prop_1" means that whenever prop_0 becomes true, at that point or in any subsequent point in time, prop_1 must eventually become true. This is a common pattern for expressing a 'whenever X, eventually Y' relationship, where X is the trigger (prop_0) and Y is the eventual outcome (prop_1). The 'G' (globally) operator ensures that this condition holds throughout the entire execution path. The '->' (implication) operator establishes the conditional relationship. The 'F' (eventually) operator indicates that prop_1 must become true at some point in the future (including the current state).

The phrase "Once P, Q" typically means that eventually P becomes true, and from that point onwards, Q will eventually become true. In this case, 'P' is 'prop_0' and 'Q' is 'prop_1'. Thus, we need a point in time where 'prop_0' is true, and from that same point, 'prop_1' must eventually be true. This is precisely captured by the LTL formula F (prop_0 & F prop_1). The phrase 'all prop_0' simply refers to the atomic proposition 'prop_0' being true.

The phrase "Once prop_0, it shall be possible to prop_1" means that whenever `prop_0` becomes true at any point in time, it must be the case that `prop_1` eventually becomes true from that point onwards. This is captured by the LTL formula `G(prop_0 -> F prop_1)`, which translates to "Globally, if `prop_0` is true, then eventually `prop_1` will be true."

The phrase "Once prop_0" implies that at any point in time where `prop_0` becomes true, a certain condition must then be met. The phrase "it shall be possible to prop_1" means that `prop_1` must eventually occur from that point onwards. Therefore, we use the Global operator (G) to ensure this rule applies at all times. Inside, we use an implication: if `prop_0` is true (antecedent), then `prop_1` must eventually be true (consequent, using the Eventually operator F).
